reply.proposal: First, audit all existing hand-rolled middleware in packages/api to document current auth, rate limiting, and logging implementations and their implicit ordering dependencies. Second, replace the ad-hoc composition with Hono's explicit, composable middleware chain using app.use() and route groups to enforce deterministic order (logging -> rate-limiting -> auth). Third, extract each concern into an isolated, unit-tested middleware: hono/jwt for auth, a Redis-backed store via @hono-rate-limiter/redis for rate limiting, and hono/logger piped to pino for structured request logging, with Zod schemas for typed context augmentation. Finally, migrate routes incrementally via a compatibility adapter, add integration tests that assert middleware order, and document the canonical chain.

Create a centralized store in apps/dashboard/src/store using configureStore from Redux Toolkit with typed setup. Identify the current sprawled global states (e.g., auth/session, dashboard filters, widget layout) and extract each into a dedicated slice via createSlice, leaving purely local UI state in useState. Wrap the dashboard root layout with react-redux Provider and expose typed hooks useAppDispatch and useAppSelector for safe usage. Migrate incrementally from prop-drilling/context to selectors/dispatches and add basic reducer tests to prevent regression. Enable Redux DevTools in development and optionally add redux-persist for filter preferences.

Run `npx prisma init` inside `packages/api` to create `prisma/schema.prisma` with `provider = "postgresql"` and `client` generator configured for TypeScript output. Define our data models in the schema by either manually porting existing tables or using `prisma db pull` to introspect, all driven by `DATABASE_URL` from `.env`. Create a singleton client at `packages/api/src/lib/prisma.ts` using `globalThis` caching to prevent multiple client instances in dev/hot-reload. Add scripts `prisma:generate`, `prisma:migrate`, and `prisma:studio` to `packages/api/package.json` and wire `prisma generate` into the build step and `migrate deploy` into CI/deploy. Incrementally replace all raw SQL string queries in the API layer with typed `prisma.*` calls to get full type-safety and autocomplete.

Create a dedicated typed BillingClient class in packages/api/src/clients/billing/ that encapsulates all provider calls instead of using fetch inline. Implement it on top of Node's native fetch (undici) with AbortSignal.timeout() for a 5s per-request timeout and 10s overall deadline. Add resilient retries using p-retry with exponential backoff + jitter for 429 and 5xx on idempotent requests only, capped at 3 retries, and validate all responses with zod schemas. Map failures to typed domain errors like BillingTimeoutError, BillingRateLimitError, BillingUpstreamError with structured pino logs including correlationId/requestId and emit metrics/traces to our existing observability stack for error reporting.

The session token should not be made readable from JavaScript. Instead, the server should set it as an HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Lax or Strict cookie. The SPA should use fetch with credentials: 'include' or 'same-origin' so the browser automatically attaches the cookie to API calls, without any JS storage or manual header injection. This eliminates XSS exfiltration risk associated with localStorage/sessionStorage while still supporting authenticated fetch calls, and can be hardened with CSP and CSRF protection.

Add jest, ts-jest, and @types/jest as devDependencies directly in packages/api. Create a jest.config.ts in packages/api using ts-jest preset with testEnvironment set to 'node', rootDir set to the package, and testMatch for '**/__tests__/**/*.test.ts' and '**/?(*.)+(spec|test).ts'. Add 'test': 'jest --passWithNoTests', 'test:watch': 'jest --watch', and 'test:coverage': 'jest --coverage' scripts to packages/api/package.json. Verify the setup with a trivial handler unit test that mocks req/res to ensure isolation and no server boot is needed.

We will use co-located CSS Modules for all new marketing pages in apps/dashboard. Each component (e.g., Hero.tsx) will live alongside its own Hero.module.css file in the same directory, giving us true component-scoped styles that satisfy the designer's preference for co-location. This approach leverages Next.js built-in CSS Module support, resulting in zero runtime overhead, automatic scoping, and good performance for marketing pages. Shared design tokens (colors, spacing, typography) will be defined as CSS variables in a central tokens.css file and consumed within the modules to ensure consistency.

Define the database as declarative state using Prisma schema.prisma as the single source of truth for all models, enums, and indexes. Configure Prisma Migrate with our PostgreSQL database URL and set the output directory to prisma/migrations for version-controlled migration history. Replace hand-written SQL workflow with `prisma migrate dev` locally to auto-generate the diff SQL from schema changes and `prisma migrate deploy` in CI/CD to apply them. Add a CI check that fails if schema.prisma was modified without a corresponding generated migration file to prevent schema drift.

Introduce a GraphQL endpoint alongside the existing REST public API, initially as a Backend-for-Frontend (BFF) for the mobile clients. Mobile screens will write precise queries requesting only the 2 fields they need, eliminating overfetching and allowing aggregation of multiple resources in a single round-trip. The layer will be implemented with Apollo Server v4 federated over our existing domain services, using DataLoader for batching/caching to avoid N+1 problems. We keep the REST API for backwards compatibility and migrate mobile clients incrementally, providing fully typed hooks via GraphQL Code Generator.

cells.context:
Project conventions and prior decisions:
- Keep the API server on Hono — Hono's middleware model covers auth, logging and rate limiting with minimal overhead, and it runs identically on Node and edge runtimes
- Use Zustand for client state in the dashboard — Our global state surface is small (session, filters, toasts); Zustand covers it in ~50 lines without ceremony
- Query Postgres with the postgres.js driver and hand-written SQL — Direct SQL keeps query plans visible and avoids a runtime abstraction layer; typed row mappers live next to each query
- Use native fetch with a small retry wrapper for outbound HTTP — Node 18+ ships fetch; one 40-line wrapper gives retries, timeouts and JSON handling without a dependency
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
- Run unit tests with the built-in node:test runner — Zero test-framework dependencies; node --test runs TypeScript output directly and CI needs no transform config
- Style the dashboard with Tailwind utility classes — No runtime style computation, works with React Server Components, and design tokens live in one config
- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern
- Hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations — Every schema change is reviewed as literal SQL; forward-only keeps prod and CI history linear
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
- Structured logging with pino, one JSON line per request — Fast, structured, and the ops dashboards parse JSON lines natively
- CI runs on GitHub Actions with a single reusable workflow — One workflow file, matrix per package; no CI vendor lock beyond what the repo host already implies
- pnpm workspace for the monorepo — Strict node_modules layout catches phantom dependencies; workspace protocol keeps internal packages linked
- Token-bucket rate limiting at the API edge, per API key — Predictable burst behavior; limits configured per plan tier
- All API errors use the problem+json envelope — RFC 7807 gives clients one error shape; internal codes map to documented types
- Local development runs through docker compose — Postgres and the API start with one command; parity with the deploy image
- Prettier with the shared config; ESLint flat config for lint rules — Formatting is not reviewed; lint rules are; keeping the tools separate keeps both fast
- Feature flags read from environment variables, no flag service — A dozen flags do not justify a flag platform; env vars deploy atomically with the code
- OpenTelemetry traces exported to the collector, sampled at 10% — Vendor-neutral instrumentation; sampling keeps ingest costs flat
- User file uploads go to S3-compatible object storage, never the database — Keeps the database small and backups fast; presigned URLs offload transfer
- Zod schemas validate every API input at the boundary — One schema per endpoint doubles as the OpenAPI source of truth
- API is versioned in the URL path (/v1), breaking changes bump the version — Explicit and cache-friendly; header versioning confused two integration partners in the spike
- Each package keeps a README with run and test instructions — Onboarding doc lives next to the code it describes
- UI strings go through the i18n catalog from day one — Retrofitting i18n was a six-week project at the last company; catalog-first is cheap now

reply.proposal: Refactor all hand-rolled logic in packages/api into typed Hono MiddlewareHandler functions with (c, next) signature and a single explicit registration order in the Hono app factory in app.ts. Establish the global stack as: OpenTelemetry trace init (sampled at 10%), pino request logger that creates a child logger bound to traceId/requestId, token-bucket rate limiting per API key using postgres.js against the Postgres UNLOGGED table, then auth that reads httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies and populates c.set('session'). Route-level middleware will then handle Zod boundary validation and map all thrown errors to the problem+json envelope, ensuring consistent ordering, observability and error shape. Cover the composition with node:test unit tests per middleware and an integration test asserting header order and behavior on a /v1 request.
acknowledged_rejections: Replacing Hono with Express/Fastify/Koa/NestJS for middleware ergonomics - rejected per prior decision to keep Hono for its middleware model and Node+edge parity
Introducing Redis for rate limiting and session caching - rejected per prior decision to keep single datastore using Postgres UNLOGGED table within 15% of Redis
Replacing pino with Winston/Bunyan - rejected per prior decision to standardize on pino JSON lines

reply.proposal: Keep Zustand per the prior dashboard decision and refactor the existing monolithic store into domain slices (session, filters, toasts, plus new feature domains) using Zustand's slice pattern. Create a single bound store in apps/dashboard/src/stores with devtools and immer middleware, with each slice file owning its state, actions, and selectors. Enforce usage of shallow selectors via useShallow and colocated selector functions to prevent re-render cascades as the feature set grows. Add node:test coverage for each slice's reducers/actions and update apps/dashboard/README with the slice convention.

reply.proposal: Do not introduce Prisma in packages/api. Continue using the postgres.js driver with hand-written SQL and typed row mappers colocated with each query, which preserves visibility into query plans and avoids a runtime abstraction layer per our data-access decision. Achieve type safety by defining explicit TypeScript interfaces for mapped rows and enforcing Zod schemas at the API boundary rather than via a generated client. Keep numbered, forward-only SQL migrations as the sole schema source and keep session caching in the Postgres UNLOGGED table to maintain a single-datastore stack. If mapper boilerplate becomes painful, propose a lightweight SQL-typing helper like pgtyped in a follow-up RFC before any ORM.
acknowledged_rejections: Replacing postgres.js driver and hand-written SQL with an ORM abstraction layer
Prisma Migrate replacing hand-written numbered forward-only SQL migrations

reply.proposal: Reuse and extend the existing native fetch retry wrapper in packages/api for the billing provider integration. Use AbortController for per-request timeouts, exponential backoff with jitter for retries on 408, 429 and 5xx, and safe JSON parsing with typed errors. Wrap calls in an OpenTelemetry span, log outcomes with pino structured fields (billing_action, status_code, attempt, latency_ms), and validate response bodies with Zod before returning. Map provider errors to our standard problem+json envelope so callers get a consistent shape without adding any new HTTP client dependency.

reply.proposal: Keep session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies set by the Hono API, as previously decided. The SPA should use the existing native fetch wrapper with credentials: 'include' so the browser attaches the cookie automatically and no JavaScript ever reads the raw token. Client-side Zustand session state should only store derived, non-sensitive state like isAuthenticated and user profile, not the token itself. This preserves XSS mitigation and avoids token exfiltration risks that come with JS-accessible storage.
key_technologies: native fetch with retry wrapper and credentials:include
Postgres UNLOGGED table for session cache
acknowledged_rejections: Storing session tokens in localStorage or sessionStorage to make them readable from JavaScript
Using non-httpOnly, JavaScript-readable cookies and manually attaching via Authorization header
Letting the SPA manage raw token storage and transmission itself

reply.proposal: Do not enable auto-generated migrations. ORM abstraction layer (Prisma, Drizzle ORM, TypeORM, Sequelize) replacing postgres.js direct SQL
Stopping hand-writing migration SQL in favor of schema-first generation

reply.proposal: Implement sparse fieldsets on the existing REST API running on Hono via ?fields=id,name,avatar to let mobile clients request only the fields they need. Validate the fields param at the boundary with Zod against a per-resource allowlist and surface the allowlist in the OpenAPI spec. Update hand-written postgres.js queries to SELECT only the requested columns with typed row mappers, so we save both DB I/O and payload size. This preserves HTTP caching, existing auth/rate-limiting middleware, and problem+json errors while solving overfetching without a new query language.
acknowledged_rejections: GraphQL - prior decision is Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec; sparse fieldsets were explicitly chosen to cover overfetching complaints

Project decision log:
- Keep the API server on Hono — Hono's middleware model covers auth, logging and rate limiting with minimal overhead, and it runs identically on Node and edge runtimes
  - REJECTED: Express — Larger dependency surface and slower cold starts; the middleware ecosystem is tempting but everything we need exists for Hono. Migration was evaluated and declined 2026-03
  - REJECTED: Fastify — No measured gain over Hono for our workload; migration cost with zero user-visible benefit
- Use Zustand for client state in the dashboard — Our global state surface is small (session, filters, toasts); Zustand covers it in ~50 lines without ceremony
  - REJECTED: Redux Toolkit — Boilerplate and indirection outweigh benefits at our state size; the team spent more time on slices than features in the spike
  - REJECTED: MobX — Implicit reactivity made renders hard to trace during the spike
- Query Postgres with the postgres.js driver and hand-written SQL — Direct SQL keeps query plans visible and avoids a runtime abstraction layer; typed row mappers live next to each query
  - REJECTED: Prisma — Query engine adds cold-start latency on serverless deploys and hides generated SQL; we hit both in the 2026-02 spike
  - REJECTED: TypeORM — Decorator-based entities fought our functional codebase style
- Use native fetch with a small retry wrapper for outbound HTTP — Node 18+ ships fetch; one 40-line wrapper gives retries, timeouts and JSON handling without a dependency
  - REJECTED: axios — Interceptor chains obscured a double-retry bug in 2026-01; native fetch plus our wrapper is smaller and easier to audit
  - REJECTED: request — Deprecated upstream since 2020; unpatched vulnerabilities
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
  - REJECTED: localStorage — XSS exfiltration — the 2026-04 pentest demonstrated token theft through a compromised analytics snippet reading localStorage
  - REJECTED: sessionStorage — Same XSS exposure as localStorage, plus tokens lost on every new tab
- Run unit tests with the built-in node:test runner — Zero test-framework dependencies; node --test runs TypeScript output directly and CI needs no transform config
  - REJECTED: Jest — ESM support still needs transform workarounds; config drift across packages burned two days in the 2026-03 attempt
  - REJECTED: Vitest — Capable, but node:test covers our needs without another dependency to track
- Style the dashboard with Tailwind utility classes — No runtime style computation, works with React Server Components, and design tokens live in one config
  - REJECTED: styled-components — Runtime CSS-in-JS is incompatible with React Server Components and added ~12KB to the bundle in the spike
  - REJECTED: CSS Modules — Workable but splits styling across files; team preferred colocated utilities
- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern

  - REJECTED: in-process memory cache — Breaks under multiple API instances; sticky sessions rejected as deploy constraint

  - REJECTED: down migrations — False confidence — most destructive changes cannot actually be reversed; restore-from-backup is the real rollback

Establish and enforce a single canonical order (request-id -> pino logger -> token-bucket rate limiter -> auth -> Zod validation) via a shared `buildApp()` that composes middleware in `packages/api`, so route files only add handlers. Add ordering regression tests with node:test that assert 401/429 short-circuits and log fields without needing extra dependencies.

reply.proposal: Do not introduce Prisma in packages/api; keep the existing postgres.js driver with hand-written SQL and typed row mappers colocated with each query. That stack keeps query plans visible, avoids the query-engine cold-start latency and hidden SQL hit from the 2026-02 spike, and aligns with our hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations. reply.proposal: Reuse the existing packages/api native fetch wrapper with its 40-line retry/timeout/JSON handling for the billing provider client instead of adding a new dependency. Configure it with billing-specific settings (5s timeout via AbortController, max 3 retries with exponential backoff and jitter on 429 and 5xx, only retrying idempotent methods) and inject base URL and API key from environment variables. Validate all provider responses with Zod schemas at the boundary and log failures with pino as structured JSON including status code, attempt count, and provider error code. Translate provider errors into our standard problem+json envelope with documented codes for rate-limited, provider-unavailable, and payment-failed cases.

reply.proposal: Keep session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies issued by the Hono API as per the current security decision — the SPA never reads the raw token. The existing native fetch retry wrapper should be configured with credentials: 'include' so the browser attaches the cookie automatically to all /v1 requests, removing the need for JS to set an Authorization header. Auth UI state (logged-in, user profile) is kept in-memory only in Zustand and derived from a /v1/me or session-check endpoint, revalidated on load, not persisted from a token. If a specific mobile-web fetch client requires header auth, route it through a same-origin BFF endpoint that translates the cookie to a request, rather than making the token readable to JS.
key_technologies: native fetch with retry wrapper and credentials:include
Zustand for in-memory session UI state
Postgres UNLOGGED table for session lookup cache

sessionStorage - Same XSS exposure as localStorage, plus tokens lost on every new tab
Non-httpOnly JavaScript-readable cookie - Same XSS theft vector as localStorage, directly contradicts httpOnly decision

reply.proposal: We cannot enable auto-generated migrations from a schema definition. Per the 2026-03 incident where a generated diff silently included a column drop that reached staging, the team decision is that hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations remain the source of truth and generated DDL is not trusted. To address the friction behind the request, we will add a pnpm script to scaffold a numbered migration file from a template and a CI check that applies all migrations to an ephemeral Postgres (docker compose) and diffs the resulting schema against a checked-in full schema.sql snapshot. This keeps review as literal SQL with linear history while reducing manual boilerplate, without putting a generator in the critical path.

reply.proposal: Extend the existing REST /v1 endpoints with a `fields` query parameter for sparse fieldsets as already intended in the API design. Clients send ?fields=id,name,avatar_url and get only those keys, keeping HTTP caching and URL versioning intact. Validate the parameter with Zod against a per-resource allowlist derived from the OpenAPI spec, then project the response in the Hono handler (and optionally narrow the SELECT list in our hand-written postgres.js queries for large tables). This solves the mobile overfetching complaint without a new query language or extra infrastructure, and remains backward compatible for existing consumers.

reply.proposal: Reuse the established native fetch with our small retry wrapper for all billing provider calls in packages/api, per our outbound HTTP standard. Instrument every call with structured logging and OpenTelemetry spans for error reporting and alerting.

reply.proposal: Keep session tokens in httpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Lax cookies so they remain invisible to JavaScript and immune to XSS exfiltration demonstrated in the April pentest. The SPA should not store or manually attach tokens; instead configure the global fetch wrapper to use credentials: 'same-origin' (or 'include') and let the browser attach the cookie automatically. For state-changing requests, rely on SameSite=Lax plus a separate CSRF defense (e.g., double-submit CSRF cookie) rather than exposing the session token to JS. This preserves our current session flow with no client-side token handling and no new storage mechanism. We should explicitly deny the mobile web team's request to make the session token readable from JavaScript.

  - REJECTED: MobX — Implicit reactivity made renders hard to trace during the spike
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
  - REJECTED: localStorage — XSS exfiltration — the 2026-04 pentest demonstrated token theft through a compromised analytics snippet reading localStorage
  - REJECTED: sessionStorage — Same XSS exposure as localStorage, plus tokens lost on every new tab
- Token-bucket rate limiting at the API edge, per API key — Predictable burst behavior; limits configured per plan tier

Relevant prior decisions for this task (from team memory):
- Hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations — Every schema change is reviewed as literal SQL; forward-only keeps prod and CI history linear
  - REJECTED: auto-generated migrations — A generated diff silently included a column drop that reached staging in 2026-03 — rolled back; generated DDL is no longer trusted for schema changes
  - REJECTED: down migrations — False confidence — most destructive changes cannot actually be reversed; restore-from-backup is the real rollback
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
  - REJECTED: GraphQL — Resolver N+1s and cache invalidation complexity outweigh flexibility for our ~30-endpoint surface; evaluated 2026-05 with the mobile team present
  - REJECTED: tRPC — Public consumers are not all TypeScript; locks the contract to one language ecosystem
- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern
  - REJECTED: Redis — A second datastore to provision, monitor, back up and secure — ops budget decision is one datastore until scale forces otherwise (revisit at 10x traffic)
  - REJECTED: in-process memory cache — Breaks under multiple API instances; sticky sessions rejected as deploy constraint
- Query Postgres with the postgres.js driver and hand-written SQL — Direct SQL keeps query plans visible and avoids a runtime abstraction layer; typed row mappers live next to each query
  - REJECTED: Prisma — Query engine adds cold-start latency on serverless deploys and hides generated SQL; we hit both in the 2026-02 spike
  - REJECTED: TypeORM — Decorator-based entities fought our functional codebase style
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
  - REJECTED: localStorage — XSS exfiltration — the 2026-04 pentest demonstrated token theft through a compromised analytics snippet reading localStorage
  - REJECTED: sessionStorage — Same XSS exposure as localStorage, plus tokens lost on every new tab

reply.proposal: We will not enable auto-generated migrations from the schema definition and will retain hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations as the sole source of truth. The schema definition can remain as documentation and input for typed row mappers, but it will not generate DDL. To reduce friction, we will add a tiny CLI that scaffolds the next numbered .sql file and a CI check that enforces linear numbering and applies migrations to an ephemeral Postgres instance. This keeps every schema change reviewable as literal SQL and prevents a repeat of the 2026-03 incident where a generated diff silently dropped a column and reached staging.

reply.proposal: Keep the public API as REST under /v1 with OpenAPI spec and solve overfetching with opt-in sparse fieldsets via `?fields=id,name,avatar`. Server enforces an allowlist per resource, supports comma-separated top-level fields with dot-notation for one-level nesting (e.g. org.name), and returns 400 for unknown fields. Document allowed fields and examples in OpenAPI, include the fields query in the HTTP cache key, and default to full resource when param is absent for backward compatibility. This gives mobile screens exact field control while preserving HTTP cacheability, simple auth, and multi-language clients.
key_technologies: REST API with URL path versioning (/v1)
Sparse fieldsets via fields query parameter (JSON:API style)
HTTP caching with cache-key including query params
